coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots prepared from glucose as optical sensor for glucose
coredata.dc:creator: Shehab, Mona
Ebrahim, Shaker
Soliman, Moataz
coredata.dc:description: Quantum Dots (QDs) show promise materials for many technological applications. In this work we utilized a simple route to prepare graphene quantum dots (GQDs) using glucose carbonization. GQDs functionalized with phenylboronic acid receptors were employed as a sensing material for a nonenzymatic glucose sensor. Photoluminance spectra of GQDs were used as a property of optical sensor for glucose. GQDs considered as a good sensing probe because of its low toxicity, high photoluminance, water solubility and excelent photochemical properties. The prepared GQDs were characterized using UV-visible, Raman and photoluminance spectroscopies, X-ray diffraction and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). HRTEM micrographs confirmed the preparation of 7–10nm GQDs and the emission peak of the GQDs appeared at 450nm. The developed sensor has linear response to glucose over a concentration range of 4–40mM with a correlation coefficient of 0.97 and a low detection limit of approximately 3.0mM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Graphene quantum dots
Optical sensor
Photoluminance
Glucose sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PL spectra for GQDs and PBS-GQDs
Selectivity of the prepared GQDs- PBS glucose sensor
Detection of glucose in real blood serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of GQDs formation from glucose pyrolysis.
UV-Visible spectrum of GQDs in water.
(a,b) HRTEM images of GQDs prepared from direct pyrolysis of glucose, (c) (SAED) of the GQDs, (d) TEM image of the PBS-GQDs in the presence of 40mM glucose represents.
PL spectra of GQDs aqueous solutions with different concentrations of PBS (from bottom to top: 0, 500, 1500 and 2000 ppm), λex=350nm.
Representation scheme of the proposed enhancement and quenching mechanism of GQDs fluorescence.
PL spectra of (GQDs+PBS) aqueous solutions with different concentrations of glucose (from top to bottom : 0, 4, 6, 10, 15, 20, 25 and 40mM), λex=350nm of glucose.
Linear plot of the PL peak intensity quenching of GQDs as a function of glucose concentration.

Relative intensity of the fluorescence (F-F0)/(FG-F0) of different saccharides. F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of 40mM Saccharides, respectively. FG represents the fluorescence intensity with 40mM glucose.
Detection of glucose in real blood serum samples.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots prepared from glucose as optical sensor for glucose

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Science Department, Institute of Graduate Studies & Research, Alexandria University, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Materials Science Department, Institute of Graduate Studies & Research, Alexandria University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: General Bureau of Beheira Governorate, Damanhour, Beheira 22111, Egypt

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Institute of Graduate Studies and Research, Alexandria University, PO Box 832, 163 Horreya Avenue, El-Shatby, Alexandria, Egypt.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Graduate Studies and Research, Alexandria University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quantum Dots (QDs) show promise materials for many technological applications. In this work we utilized a simple route to prepare graphene quantum dots (GQDs) using glucose carbonization. GQDs functionalized with phenylboronic acid receptors were employed as a sensing material for a nonenzymatic glucose sensor. Photoluminance spectra of GQDs were used as a property of optical sensor for glucose. GQDs considered as a good sensing probe because of its low toxicity, high photoluminance, water solubility and excelent photochemical properties. The prepared GQDs were characterized using UV-visible, Raman and photoluminance spectroscopies, X-ray diffraction and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). HRTEM micrographs confirmed the preparation of 7–10nm GQDs and the emission peak of the GQDs appeared at 450nm. The developed sensor has linear response to glucose over a concentration range of 4–40mM with a correlation coefficient of 0.97 and a low detection limit of approximately 3.0mM.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes is a metabolic disorder, indicated by blood glucose concentrations, and causes with long-term damage and failure of various organs like the heart, kidney, eyes, and blood vessels [1]. The early and sensitive detection of abnormal glucose level in the blood is very important for proper treatment to reduce many health problems [2,3]. Several approaches have been devoted to fabricate the glucose biosensors based on glucose oxidase enzyme. However, the immobilization of enzyme on the electrode surface is a very challenging task, and it is highly affected by temperature, pH, humidity, which causes poor stability [4]. Consequently, nonenzymatic glucose sensors are preferred and have been widely explored and developed [5].

With the highly developed nanotechniques, many sensitive, stable and cost-effective biosensors using different nanomaterials have been reported [6]. Luminescent semiconductor nanocrystals, CdS (Myung et al., 2002) [7], CdTe (Bae et al., 2004) [8] and CdSe/ZnSe (Myung et al., 2003) [9] which is referred to as (QDs), used for a wide variety of promising applications in optoelectronic devices, biomedicine and biological labeling. Although these semiconductor QDs have been accepted as electrochemiluminescence (ECL) indicators for their good and valuable ECL behavior, these heavy metals QDs cause a serious health and environmental hazardous [10].

GQDs are attracting considerable attention because they can be replaced the traditional QDs due to their chemical inertness, low toxicity biocompatibility, eco-friendly, high fluorescent activity, stable photoluminescence [11], good solubility, better surface grafting, thus making them suitable for optoelectronic devices, sensors, bioimaging, etc. [12,13]. Graphene, a single layer of densely packed carbon atoms in honeycomb two-dimensional (2D) lattice, has extensive physical and chemical applications, owing to its unique electronic, thermal, mechanical and chemical properties [14,15]. No optical luminescence is observed in graphene as there is no band gap (Loh et al., 2010) [16]. However, it has been experimentally proven and theoretically predicted that the properties of graphene sheets can effectively determine by the sheets morphology, including size, shape and thickness [17]. To effectively tune the band gap of graphene, promising approach is to convert the 2D graphene sheets into 0D GQDs. GQDs, as recently emerging carbon nanomaterials, are graphene sheets smaller than 10nm [18,19]. They exhibit new phenomena due to quantum confinement and edge effects [20].

As graphene exhibits an infinite exciton Bohr radius, quantum confinement may take effect in graphene of any finite size, which expected to have many interesting phenomena [21]. Graphene can be widely used for photodetectors over a broad spectrum range due to its large absorption in the THz and IR range and its broadband absorption of 2.3% from the mid-infrared to ultraviolet [22]. Dong et al. prepared blue luminescent GQDs as well as GO by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid and reported that the GQDs were well surface-passivated by the incompletely carbonized citric acid [13].

The preparation of GQDs can be developed by both top–down and bottom–up approaches. However, top–down method has some disadvantages, such as low yield, the requirement of special equipment, critical synthesis conditions, or difficulty in controlling the size distribution of products. On the other hand, the bottom–up strategy, namely carbonizing some special organic precursors by thermal treatment, usually allows precise control over the morphology and the size distribution of the products. However, those organic precursors used in these methods need complicated synthesis procedures, and are difficult to be obtained [23].

Preparation of GQDs from hexa-peri-hexabenzocoronene pyrolysis was reported by Liu et al. [24]. Further, GQDs from citric acid and glutathione as starting materials were also prepared [25]. In another approach, Wu and coworkers synthesized GQDs using a simple one-step pyrolysis of L-glutamic acid in a heating mantle [26]. Gram-scale synthesis of functionalized GQDs from pyrene via facile molecular fusion route was reported by Wang et al. [27]. Also facile bottom-up method producing fluorescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots based on one-step pyrolysis of citric acid and tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane was reported [28].

In this work a simple bottom-up method to obtain water-soluble GQDs based on directly carbonization of glucose as a new and available organic precursor was reported. The as-prepared product exhibited bright blue emission under ultraviolet irradiation (∼350nm) in a water solution of neutral pH. In our study phenyl boronic salt (PBS) are used as a receptor because it is able to form covalent complexes with glucose in the range of 4–40mM which is suited for practical use with the human serum.

Glucose, sucrose, galactose, fructose and lactose were purchased from El Nasr Pharm. Chem. Co. (Adwic). Ammonia solution 25% was purchased from Chem Solute (Germany). Sodium hydroxide was obtained from Carlo Erba reagents and PBA 98+% was received from Acros Organic. All chemicals were used as received without additional purification.

The GQDs was prepared by directly glucose pyrolysis as shown in the schematic representation of Fig. (1). For the preparation of GQDs, 2g of glucose was placed into a beaker and heated to 250°C using a hot plate. About 5min later, the glucose was liquated. Subsequently, the color of the liquid was changed from colorless to yellow, and then orange in 20min. The obtained orange liquid for preparing GQDs was added drop by drop into 100mL of 12.5% ammonia solution, under vigorous stirring. Then the solution was heated at 70°C for 3h until the odor of ammonia disappeared and the solution neutralized to pH 7.0.

UV-visible spectroscopy of GQDs solution was performed with UV-Visible spectrophotometer Thermo USA (Evolution 600). The X-Ray diffraction patterns of the samples were recorded on Shimadzu-Japan (7000) diffractometer. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was performed using JEOL (JEM-2100 LaB6) Japan, which is a good tool to study the particle size, morphology and crystal structure. Raman spectra were measured with Raman spectroscopy microscope (Senterra Bruker). Zeta potential measurements were performed using a Zetasizer Malvern Nano-ZS. Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were carried out at room temperature with a (Perkin Elmer LS-55) spectrometer.

For the glucose detection, the following was added sequentially to a Falcon 15mL conical centrifuge tubes: 3ml of GQDs solution (10mL from the as prepared GQD solution in 200ml DI water), 2ml of (PBS) solution (10mgml−1) prepared by the addition of 0.5g PBA to 50ml of 0.1M NaOH and different amounts of glucose solution (1M). This was then diluted with DI water to a volume of 10mL. The reaction mixture was then incubated at room temperature for 10min, and the PL spectra of the resulting solution were recorded by fitting the excitation wavelengths at 350.0nm. For selectivity measurements the glucose solution was replaced by lactose, galactose, sucrose and fructose solutions and a local medical laboratory provides us with 4 specimens of blood, S1–S4, as well as their clinical glucose level data, which were obtained by the standard clinical technique. For the PL measurements of the blood serum samples 100µl of blood serum was added to 300µl of the previously prepared GQDs solution, 0.2µl PBS and 400µl DI water.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs in water ( Fig. 2) shows an absorption band at ~ 270 of the π-π* transition peak which is characteristic for GQDs. However, two kinds of characteristic features usually observed in spectra of GO, the first is a shoulder at ∼310nm, corresponding to an n-π* plasmon peak. Another characteristic feature of GO appears at ~ 230nm, and corresponds to a π-π* plasmon peak [29].

Fig. 3(a, b) displays the typical HRTEM images of as-prepared GQDs, showing a size distribution between 7 and 10nm and the obvious crystal lattice presents high crystallinity of GQDs with the lattice parameter of 0.24nm. Fig. 3(c) shows the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) of the GQDs which shows the six fold symmetry crystalline structure of the GQDs. HRTEM image of the PBS-GQDs in the presence of 40mM glucose represents in Fig. 3(d) and it is observed that there is an aggregation of the glucose bound PBS-GQDs.

Raman spectroscopy ( Fig. 4) confirms the quality of the as-prepared GQDs. The major Raman features are the D band at around 1352cm−1, the G band at around 1587cm− 1 and 2D band at around 2200cm−1. The relative intensity of the “disorder” D-band and the crystalline G-band ( I D / I G ) for as prepared GQDs in this work is only around 0.6, similar to that of high quality few-layer graphene nanoribbons, which indicates the high quality of as-prepared GQDs [30].The peak at 1112cm−1 is due to C–H bending modes in hydrocarbons.

XRD pattern ( Fig. 5) of GQDs presents a wide (002) peak d=0.34nm at 2⊖ = 26°. The observed broad peak also indicates that the prepared GQDs have a small particle size, and it differs than that of the crystalline graphite. The absence of any peak characterized for graphene oxide which is a sharp peak usually appears around 2θ=19° indicate that the prepared sample is pure GQDs [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL spectra for GQDs and PBS-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The PL spectrum of as prepared GQDs obtained at an excitation wavelength of 350nm is shown in Fig. (6). The PL spectrum of as prepared GQDs shows an emission peak in the visible spectrum at 426nm. The PL in this carbon system is due to a consequence of geminate recombination of localized electron–hole pairs in sp2 clusters which essentially behave as luminescent centers [32]. Tunable PL can be achieved by controlling the sp2 sites since the band gap depends on the size, shape and the fraction of sp2 domains. The PL intensity increases with an increase in sp2 content in the disordered carbon systems [33,34]. The aqueous GQDs suspension is yellow–brown in color and transparent in the daylight but emits intense blue–green luminescence under irradiation with a 350nm lamp [35].

Fig. (6) shows the effect of the addition of different concentrations of PBS which increase the intensity of the PL peak till we reach PBS concentration of 2000 ppm then the further addition has no effect on the peak intensity and this is attributed to the sites on GQDs surface are occupied. The fluorescence enhancement after the addition of PBS is caused to the coupled effect between conjugated aromatic rings of GQDs and PBS functional groups as indicated in the schematic diagram of Fig. (7). The high fluorescence of PBS-GQDs is beneficial for sensitive detection applications [36].

It was found that the addition of glucose into the aqueous solution of GQDs-functionalized with PBS led to the PL quenching of the solution. Fig. (8) presents the PL spectra of the prepared solution at various concentrations of glucose. With increasing concentration of glucose, the PL intensity gradually decreases and a red-shift of about 62meV (9nm) were observed. The energy difference is an indication of cluster–cluster interaction in a closely packed layer of GQDs [37].

As the original PL peak intensity varies among the studied samples, it is more appropriate to plot a change in the PL peak intensity after the glucose treatment and is normalized to the original peak intensity as shown in Fig. (9) according to Stern-Volmer equation F0/F = 1+ Ksv [Q], where F0 and F are the PL intensities observed in the absence and presence of quencher respectively, KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and [Q] is the concentration of quencher. Such a plot as a function of the concentration is often called a calibration curve [38]. A good linearity is obtained in the glucose concentration range of 4–40mM, corresponding to 72–720mg/dL with a good line fit with a correlation coefficient of 0.97 and sensitivity of 0.012mM−1. Since the glucose concentrations of the human serum is 80–120mg/dL or 4.4–6.6mM and higher for diabetics, the sensing range of the prepared sensor is well suited as a glucose sensor for practical use [39,40]. The calibration equation of Y = −0.0124X + 0.9598 (Y=F/F0, X=concentration; R2=0.9761). The theoretical linear range from 3.2 to 77.4mM and a low detection limit of approximately 3.0mM or lower the detection limit (DL) can be estimated from the equation of DL=3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of the response and S is the slope of the calibration curve [3]. Comparing with boronic acid–quantum dot hybrid sensing systems reported previously, the linearity range of the calibration curve by Qu et al. [41] of aminobenzeneboronic acid -GQDs was 0.1mM to 10mM and the detection limit of 5.0mM or lower so in this work we increase the linearity range and enhance the detection limit and it will be suitable for more glucose sensing applications in a wide range.

However, similar to Carbon Dots (CDs), the PL mechanism of GQDs is still a matter of current debate and requires further clarification. Typically, the luminescence mechanism may derive from intrinsic state emission and defect state emission [42]. The former is induced by quantum size effect [11,30], zigzag edge sites [43,44] and recombination of localized electron–hole pairs [16], while the latter arises from defect effect (energy traps) [11,45]. All these potential mechanisms show a high level of convergence with that for CDs as listed above. As mentioned in the introduction section, GQDs exhibit extraordinary quantum confinement and edge effects, which can play an important role in the PL of GQDs [13].

A surface quenching states (SQS) induced mechanism [37] is proposed. Surface passivated by oxygen-rich functional groups, the PBS-GQDs are all negatively charged and this structural changes were proved through the zeta potential; the zeta potential of as-prepared GQDs is about −24mV, while the zeta potential of the GQDs with 2000 ppm PBS is only −9mV. The glucose molecules bound to PBS groups form negatively charged boronate complexes with zeta potential of −11mV as shown in the scheme represents in Fig. (7). Coulomb repulsion is happened between these PBS-GQDs. On the other hand, PBS-GQDs are crosslinked by covalent anchored glucose. Electrostatic repulsion and covalent cross-linking would construct a counterbalance of aggregation and disaggregation of the glucose bound PBS-GQDs. An elastic tension introduced by the competing repulsive and retractive forces may stretch the interfaces of the PBS-GQDs to form surface states for efficient fluorescent quenching. TEM images show the aggregation of the glucose bound PBS-GQDs. Cross-linked PBS-GQDs can be distinguished in (Fig. 3b) with 40mM glucose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the prepared GQDs- PBS glucose sensor
ce:section.ce:para: In order to confirm the that PBS-GQDs can be used as a glucose sensor for the diagnosis of diabetes, interference in sensing from other substances present in human serum was studied before testing the PBS-GQDs glucose sensor with human serum. It was found that PBS-GQDs can recognize glucose amongst four other saccharides e.g. fructose, galactose, sucrose and lactose.

It can be observed from Fig. (10) that disaccharides have a quite different effect on the fluorescence of PBS-GQDs from that of monosaccharides. Sucrose and lactose tend to enhance the fluorescence of PBS-GQDs rather than to quench it. It is noted that the affinity constants (Ka) for binding of the other two monosaccharides (fructose and galactose) to the PBS-GQDs are larger than that of glucose [46]. This suggests that the binding affinity strength is not the definitive parameter in the selective fluorescent detection of glucose. For example, fructose has no additional cis conformational diol unit to further bind with PBS-GQDs to form SQS-contained aggregation though it is easier to covalently combine with PBS-GQDs than glucose. Similarly with the case for fructose, galactose has no more cis-diol units to quench the fluorescence of PBS-GQDs by aggregation. That is why the PBS-GQDs show high selectivity for glucose. In disaccharides, they have more hydroxyl groups and molecular flexibility, which offers the possibility to form intramolecular cross-linking architectures. An enhancing effect can be caused by restriction of the intramolecular rotations of the boronic acid groups in the intramolecular cross-linking architectures. This results indicates that the PBS-GQDs has highly selective and sensitive detection for glucose in aqueous solution, which is proper for real sample determination [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glucose in real blood serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: We examine the feasibility of a PBS-GQDs glucose sensor using actual human blood samples. A local medical laboratory provides us with 4 specimens of blood, S1–S4, as well as their clinical glucose level data, which were obtained by a certain clinical technique. The PL spectra of the specimens were measured from which the glucose concentrations were determined using the calibration curve in Fig. (9). The obtained glucose concentrations and the clinical data provided by the medical laboratory are shown in Table (1), the recoveries were in the range 93.6–98%. It is noted that normal persons maintain their glucose concentration within very narrow limits (80–120mg/dL or 4.4–6.6mM) [40]. The glucose concentrations of the blood serum samples include both of normal persons and diabetic patients. The glucose concentrations determined by the PL quenching were slightly lower than those provided by the medical laboratory. The results of testing a PBS-GQDs glucose sensor with real human blood serum have convinced us that the PBS-GQDs sensor based on the PL quenching can be used as a detector of glucose concentrations in human blood.

We have synthesized photoluminescence sensor consist of phenylboronic salt functionalized GQDs for detection of glucose molecule. PBS functionalized GQD based sensor was used for detection of glucose in the range of 4–40mM, corresponding to 72–720mg/dL. This fluorescent based sensor was easily synthesized and can be stored for a period of long time for the detection based application. The developed sensor has linear response to glucose with a correlation coefficient of 0.97 and a low detection limit of approximately 3.0mM.
